name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَّن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ نَصِيبٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ كِفْلٌ مِّنْهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا (٨٥)
```

```english
Whoever intercedes for a good cause shall receive a share of it, and whoever intercedes for an evil cause shall share its burden, and Allah is prepotent over all things. (85)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا حُيِّيتُم بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا۟ بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْهَآ أَوْ رُدُّوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَسِيبًا (٨٦)
```

```english
When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it, or return it; indeed Allah takes account of all things. (86)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۗ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حَدِيثًا (٨٧)
```

```english
Allah—there is no god except Him—will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt; and who is more truthful in speech than Allah? (87)
```

```arabic
۞ فَمَا لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَٱللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَهْدُوا۟ مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا (٨٨)
```

```english
Why should you be two groups concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them relapse [into unfaith] because of their deeds? Do you desire to guide someone Allah has led astray? Whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him. (88)
```

```arabic
وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَكُونُونَ سَوَآءً ۖ فَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٨٩)
```

```english
They are eager that you should disbelieve like they have disbelieved, so that you all become alike. So do not make friends [with anyone] from among them, until they migrate in the way of Allah. But if they turn their backs, seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and do not take from among them friends or helpers, (89)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ أَوْ جَآءُوكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقَـٰتَلُوكُمْ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ فَمَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا (٩٠)
```

```english
excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you, or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have imposed them upon you, and then they would have surely fought you. So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any course [of action] against them. (90)
```

```arabic
سَتَجِدُونَ ءَاخَرِينَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَيَأْمَنُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ كُلَّ مَا رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَيُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ وَيَكُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ جَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (٩١)
```

```english
You will find others desiring to be secure from you, and secure from their own people; yet whenever they are called back to polytheism, they relapse into it. So if they do not keep out of your way, nor offer you peace, nor keep their hands off [from fighting], then seize them and kill them wherever you confront them, and it is such against whom We have given you a clear sanction. (91)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, as they all (85–91) address matters related to fighting a group of polytheists (المشركين), specifically the hypocrites (المنافقون) among them. It appears from contemplating them that they were revealed concerning a group of polytheists who displayed faith (الإيمان) to the believers, then returned to their home and joined the polytheists in their polytheism (شركهم). Consequently, doubt arose regarding fighting them, and the views of the Muslims differed concerning their affair. Some argued for fighting them, while others forbade it and interceded for them because of their outward display of faith. Allah, the Glorified, then prescribed for them either emigration (المهاجرة) or combat, and warned the believers against interceding on their behalf.

They are followed by another group, and then another, for whom it is prescribed to either offer peace (إلقاء السلم) or face combat. To introduce the objectives contained in the verses, the discourse begins by explaining the state of intercession (الشفاعة) in one verse, and the state of greeting (التحية) in another, due to its relevance to the offering of peace.

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ يَشْفَعْ شَفاعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُنْ لَهُ نَصِيبٌ مِنْها** — *Whoever intercedes with a good intercession shall have a share of it* (4:85). The "share" (النصيب) and the "portion" (الكفل) carry the same meaning. Since intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah or among people* — is a type of mediation to repair a deficiency or to acquire a merit and the like, it possesses a kind of causality in rectifying an affair. Thus, it carries a degree of consequence (تبعة) or reward (مثوبة) related to the purpose for which the intercession was made, which is the objective of both the intercessor and the one interceded for. Therefore, the intercessor possesses a share of the good or evil resulting from the intercession, which is His saying, the Exalted: "Whoever intercedes with an intercession..." (the verse).

Mentioning this reality serves as a reminder for the believers and a notification for them to be vigilant when interceding as to what they are interceding for. They must avoid it if the purpose of the intercession involves evil or corruption, such as interceding for the hypocrites among the polytheists so that they are not fought. Indeed, leaving a small corruption as it is and allowing it to grow and magnify results in a subsequent corruption that nothing can withstand, leading to the destruction of tilth and progeny. Thus, the verse carries the meaning of a prohibition against an evil intercession, which is the intercession for the people of oppression, tyranny, hypocrisy, and polytheism who spread corruption in the land.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا حُيِّيتُمْ بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْها** — *When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it* (4:86). This is a command to respond to a greeting (التحية) with that which exceeds it or equals it. It is a general ruling for every greeting one is greeted with, although the context of the verses specifically concerns the greeting of peace and reconciliation offered to the Muslims, as appears from the subsequent verses.

His saying, the Exalted: **اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ** — *Allah—there is no god except Him. He will surely gather you* (4:87). The meaning of the verse is clear, and it serves as a rationale for the content of the two preceding verses. It is as if it were said: Adhere to what Allah has charged you with regarding good and evil intercession, and do not nullify the greeting of one who greets you by turning away or rejecting it, for before you is a Day on which Allah will gather you and requite you for responding to what He called you toward or rejecting it.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَما لَكُمْ فِي الْمُنافِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَاللهُ أَرْكَسَهُمْ** — *Why should you be two parties concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them regress* (4:88). The "party" (الفئة) is the group, and "regression" (الإركاس) is the turning back.

The verse, given its content, is as if it branches out from the preceding introduction and preparation—namely His saying: "Whoever intercedes with an intercession..." (the verse). The meaning is: If an evil intercession gives its practitioner a portion of its wretchedness, then why is it, O believers, that you have split into two parties concerning the hypocrites and divided into two factions? One party sees the necessity of fighting them, while another party intercedes for them and urges the abandonment of fighting them, overlooking the tree of corruption that grows with their growth and bears fruit with their maturity. Allah has returned them to misguidance after they had emerged from it, as a recompense for the evil deeds they earned. Do you desire, through your intercession, to guide those whom Allah has led astray? And whomever Allah leads astray, for him you will find no way to guidance.

In His saying: **وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ سَبِيلاً** — *and whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him* (4:88), there is a transition (التفات) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This is an indication that those among the believers who intercede for them do not fully comprehend the reality of this discourse; had they understood it, they would not have interceded on their behalf. Thus, He turned away from addressing them with it and directed it to the one for whom it is clear and evident, namely the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَما كَفَرُوا فَتَكُونُونَ سَواءً** — *They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved, so that you may be all alike* (4:89). This serves as an explanation for His saying: "while Allah has made them regress because of what they have earned. Do you desire to guide those whom Allah has led astray?" The meaning is: They disbelieved, and added to that their wish and desire that you should disbelieve like them so that you become equal.

Then He forbade taking them as allies (الولاية) unless they emigrate in the way of Allah. If they turn away, then you have no obligation toward them except to seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and to avoid taking them as allies or helpers. In His saying: "But if they turn away" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا), there is an indication that the believers must charge them with emigration; if they respond, they may be allied with, but if they turn away, they are to be killed.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلى قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ أَوْ جاؤُكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ** — *excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant* (4:90). Allah, the Glorified, excepted two groups from His saying: "But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them." The first: "those who join..." (الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ) etc., meaning those between whom and some of the people of the treaty (أهل الميثاق) there is a connection that joins them, such as an alliance or the like. The second: those who feel constrained (يتحرجون) from fighting the Muslims and from fighting their own people, whether due to their killing or other factors. They withdraw from the believers and offer them peace, being neither for the believers nor against them in any way. These two groups are excepted from the aforementioned ruling. His saying: "their hearts being reluctant" (حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ) means they became constricted.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَتَجِدُونَ آخَرِينَ** — *You will find others* (4:91). This is an announcement that you will encounter another group who may resemble the second of the two excepted groups, in that they wish to be secure from you and secure from their own people. However, Allah, the Glorified, informs that they are hypocrites who are not to be trusted in their promises or their peace-making. Therefore, He replaced the two positive conditions mentioned regarding others—namely His saying: "So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace"—with the negative condition—namely His saying: "If they do not keep out of your way, nor offer you peace, nor restrain their hands" (4:91). This is in the sense of alerting the believers to be cautious of them. The meaning of the verse is clear.

## A Discourse on the Meaning of Greeting (التحية)

Nations and peoples, despite their differences in civilization, primitivity, progress, or backwardness, are never without a greeting (تحية) in their societies that they recognize when meeting one another. These greetings take various forms and types, such as nodding the head, gesturing with the hand, tipping hats, and so on. They vary according to the different factors operating within their societies.

If you contemplate these greetings circulating among nations in all their diversity, you will find them indicative of a type of submission, humility, and self-abasement shown by the lower to the higher, the lowly to the noble, the obedient to the one obeyed, and the slave to the master. In short, they reveal a custom of enslavement (الاستعباد) that has remained prevalent among nations during eras of barbarism and beyond, even if its colors have changed. For this reason, we see that such greetings begin from the obedient and end with the obeyed, starting from the lowly subordinate and concluding with the high and noble. Thus, they are among the fruits of idolatry (الوثنية) which suckles from the breast of enslavement.

Islam—as you know—has as its greatest concern the erasure of idolatry and every custom that leads to it or is born from it. Therefore, it adopted for this matter a straight path and a tradition (سنة) opposite to the tradition of idolatry and the custom of enslavement. This is the offering of peace (إلقاء السلام), which is a form of security for the one greeted from being transgressed against or having his innate human freedom (الحرية الفطرية) granted to him suppressed. The first thing required for cooperative society among individuals is that they feel secure from one another regarding their lives, honor, and property, and every matter that reverts to one of these three.

This is the "Peace" (السلام) which Allah the Exalted has legislated to be offered at every meeting between two people. The Exalted said: **فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً** — *But when you enter houses, greet yourselves with a salutation from Allah, blessed and good* (24:61). And the Exalted said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced your presence and greeted their inmates. That is better for you. Maybe you will take admonition* (24:27). Allah disciplined His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by greeting the believers, even though he is their master, saying: **وَإِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا فَقُلْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ** — *When those who have faith in Our signs come to you, say, ‘Peace be to you! Your Lord has made mercy incumbent upon Himself’* (6:54). He also commanded him to greet others in His saying: **فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ فَسوفَ يَعْلَمُونَ** — *So overlook them and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know* (43:89).

The greeting by offering peace (إلقاء السلام) was practiced among the Arabs of the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية), as evidenced by what has been transmitted from them in poetry and the like. In *Lisan al-Arab*: "The Arabs in the pre-Islamic period used to greet by one saying to his companion: 'May your morning be pleasant' (أنعم صباحا) and 'May you avoid being cursed' (أبيت اللعن). They would also say 'Peace be upon you' (سلام عليكم), as if it were a sign of peacefulness and that there was no war. Then Allah brought Islam, so they restricted it to 'Peace' (السلام) and were commanded to spread it." (End quote).

However, Allah, the Glorified, recounts it in the stories of Abraham from him (peace be upon him) frequently. This is not without evidence that it was among the remnants of the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف) of Abraham among the Arabs, like the Hajj and similar rites. The Exalted said, recounting his dialogue with his father: **قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي** — *He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you’* (19:47). And the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا إِبْراهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرى قالُوا سَلاماً قالَ سَلامٌ** — *Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news. They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said* (11:69). This story occurs in more than one place in the Noble Quran.

Allah, the Glorified, has adopted it as a greeting for Himself and used it in various instances of His speech. The Exalted said: **سَلامٌ عَلى نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ** — *‘Peace be to Noah throughout all the worlds!’* (37:79). And He said: **سَلامٌ عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ** — *‘Peace be to Abraham!’* (37:109). And He said: **سَلامٌ عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ** — *‘Peace be to Moses and Aaron!’* (37:120). And He said: **سَلامٌ عَلى إِلْ ياسِينَ** — *‘Peace be to Ilyasin!’* (37:130). And He said: **وَسَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — *And peace be to the apostles!* (37:181).

The Exalted mentioned that it is the greeting of His noble angels, saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ** — *those whom the angels take away while they are pure, saying [to them], ‘Peace be to you!’* (16:32). And He said: **وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ** — *and the angels will enter on them from every door: ‘Peace be to you!’* (13:23–24). He also mentioned that it is the greeting of the people of Paradise, saying: **وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ** — *and their greeting therein will be ‘Peace!’* (10:10). And the Exalted said: **لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً** — *They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, but only the word, ‘Peace! Peace!’* (56:25–26).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "When you are greeted..." (the verse): He said: Ali ibn Ibrahim mentioned in his *Tafsir*, from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them): "The meaning of the greeting (التحية) in the verse is peace (السلام) and other forms of righteousness (البر)."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from al-Sukuni, he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **السلام تطوع والرد فريضة** — "The greeting is voluntary, and the response is an obligation."

In the same source, with its chain from Jarrah al-Mada'ini from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: **يسلم الصغير على الكبير ، والمار على القاعد ، والقليل على الكثير** — "The younger should greet the older, the passerby should greet the one sitting, and the few should greet the many."

In the same source, with its chain from 'Uyaynah (1) from Mus'ab from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: **القليل يبدءون الكثير بالسلام ، والراكب يبدأ الماشي ، وأصحاب البغال يبدءون أصحاب الحمير ، وأصحاب الخيل يبدءون أصحاب البغال** — "The few should initiate the greeting to the many, the rider should initiate to the walker, the owners of mules should initiate to the owners of donkeys, and the owners of horses should initiate to the owners of mules."

In the same source, with its chain from Ibn Bukayr from some of his companions from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I heard him saying: **يسلم الراكب على الماشي ، والماشي على القاعد ، وإذا لقيت جماعة سلم الأقل على الأكثر ، وإذا لقي واحد جماعة سلم الواحد على الجماعة** — "The rider greets the walker, and the walker greets the one sitting. When a group meets another, the smaller group greets the larger one, and when one person meets a group, the individual greets the group."

I say: Something similar was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Bayhaqi from Zayd ibn Aslam from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the same source, with the chain from him (peace be upon him), he said: **إذا مرت الجماعة بقوم أجزأهم أن يسلم واحد منهم ، وإذا سلم على القوم وهم جماعة ، أجزأهم أن يرد واحد منهم** — "If a group passes by a people, it suffices for one of them to greet. If a greeting is given to a people who are a group, it suffices for one of them to respond."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim, he said: I entered upon Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) while he was in prayer and said: "Peace be upon you." He replied: **السلام عليك** — "Peace be upon you." I then said: "How have you reached the morning?" He remained silent. When he finished, I asked: "Does one respond to the greeting while in prayer?" He said: **نعم ، مثل ما قيل له** — "Yes, exactly as it was said to him."

In the same source, with its chain from Mansur ibn Hazim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: **إذا سلم عليك الرجل وأنت تصلي ، قال : ترد عليه خفيا كما قال** — "If a man greets you while you are praying, he said: You respond to him quietly just as he said."

In *al-Faqih*, with its chain from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him), he said: **لا تسلموا على اليهود ، ولا على النصارى ، ولا على المجوس ، ولا على عبدة الأوثان ، ولا على موائد شراب الخمر ، ولا على صاحب الشطرنج والنرد ، ولا على المخنث ، ولا على الشاعر الذي يقذف المحصنات ، ولا على المصلي لأن المصلي لا يستطيع أن يرد السلام ، لأن التسليم من المسلم تطوع والرد فريضة ، ولا على آكل الربا ، ولا على رجل جالس على غائط ـ ولا على الذي في الحمام ، ولا على الفاسق المعلن بفسقه** — "Do not greet the Jews, nor the Christians, nor the Magians, nor the idol-worshippers, nor those at tables where wine is drunk, nor the player of chess and backgammon, nor the effeminate, nor the poet who slanders chaste women, nor the one praying—because the one praying cannot respond to the greeting, for the initiation of peace by the Muslim is voluntary while the response is an obligation—nor the consumer of usury, nor a man sitting for defecation, nor the one in the bathhouse, nor the profligate who is open about his profligacy."

I say: The narrations carrying the meaning of what has preceded are numerous. Grasping the preceding explanation clarifies the meaning of the narrations. Peace (السلام) is a greeting signaling the extension of peace and the spreading of security between those meeting on the basis of equality (المساواة) and balance (التعادل), free from superiority or suppression. What is found in the narrations regarding the younger initiating the greeting to the older, the few to the many, and the individual to the group does not contradict the principle of equality. Rather, it is based on the necessity of observing rights (الحقوق). Islam did not command its followers to abolish rights or neglect the matter of virtues and merits; rather, it commanded the one without merit to observe the merit of the one possessing it and the right of the right-holder. It only forbade the one with merit from being conceited with his merit and acting arrogantly toward others, thereby transgressing against people without right, which would destroy the balance between the parts of human society.

As for the prohibition mentioned regarding greeting certain individuals, it branches out from the prohibition of taking them as allies (توليهم) or leaning toward them (الركون إليهم), as the Exalted said: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ** — *do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies* (5:51). And He said: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ** — *do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends* (60:1). And He said: **وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا** — *And do not incline toward the wrongdoers* (11:113), among other verses.

Yes, sometimes the interest of drawing near to the wrongdoers to convey the religion or to make them hear the word of truth necessitates greeting them, so that complete familiarity is achieved and souls are blended, as the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded in His saying: "So overlook them and say, ‘Peace!’" (43:89), and as in His saying describing the believers: **وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً** — *and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’* (25:63).

In *Tafsir al-Safi*, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: A man said to him: "Peace be upon you." He replied: **وعليك السلام ورحمة الله** — "And upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah." Another said: "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah." He replied: **وعليك السلام ورحمة الله وبركاته** — "And upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah and His blessings." Another said: "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah and His blessings." He replied: **وعليك** — "And upon you." The man said: "You have diminished me; where is what Allah said: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it'?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: **إنك لم تترك فضلا ورددت عليك مثله** — "You left no room for increase, so I returned to you its like." I say: Something similar was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ahmad in *al-Zuhd*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh with a good chain from Salman al-Farsi.

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) passed by a people and greeted them. They said: "Upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah, His blessings, His forgiveness, and His pleasure." The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said to them: **لا تجاوزوا بنا مثل ما قالت الملائكة لأبينا إبراهيم ، قالوا : رحمة الله وبركاته عليكم أهل البيت** — "Do not exceed for us what the angels said to our father Abraham; they said: 'May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household!'"

I say: In this is an indication that the tradition (السنة) of the complete greeting—which is the Muslim saying "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah and His blessings"—is taken from the primordial religion (الحنيفية) of Abraham (peace be upon him), and it supports what was mentioned earlier that the greeting of peace is from the primordial religion.

In the same source, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Part of the completion of the greeting for the resident is the handshake (المصافحة), and the completion of the greeting for the traveler is the embrace (المعانقة)."

In *al-Khisal*, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "When one of you sneezes, say: 'May Allah have mercy on you' (يرحمكم الله), and he should say: 'May Allah forgive you and have mercy on you' (يغفر الله لكم ويرحمكم). Allah the Exalted said: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it' (the verse)."

In *al-Manaqib*: A slave-girl brought a bunch of aromatic herbs to al-Hasan (peace be upon him). He said to her: "You are free for the sake of Allah." When questioned about this, he (peace be upon him) said: "Allah the Exalted disciplined us, saying: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it' (the verse), and the better response was her manumission."

I say: The narrations, as you see, generalize the meaning of the greeting (التحية) in the verse.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Why should you be two parties concerning the hypocrites..." (the verse): He said: They differed as to whom this verse was revealed about. It was said: It was revealed concerning a people who came to Medina from Mecca and displayed Islam to the Muslims, then returned to Mecca—because they found the climate of Medina unhealthy—and displayed polytheism. Then they traveled with the goods of the polytheists to al-Yamamah. The Muslims wanted to raid them but differed: some said, "We shall not do so, for they are believers," while others said, "They are polytheists." So Allah revealed the verse concerning them. He said: This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved..." (the verse): It was revealed concerning Ashja' and Banu Damrah, which are two tribes. The account of them is that when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went out for the campaign of al-Hudaybiyyah, he passed near their lands. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had already made a truce with Banu Damrah and made a promise to them before that. The companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O Messenger of Allah, these are Banu Damrah near us, and we fear they might act against us in Medina or aid the Quraysh against us; if only we were to start with them." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "By no means! They are the most dutiful of the Arabs to parents, the most maintaining of kinship, and the most faithful to covenants."

The lands of Ashja' were near the lands of Banu Damrah, who were a branch of Kinanah. There was an alliance between Ashja' and Banu Damrah for grazing and security. When the lands of Ashja' suffered drought and the lands of Banu Damrah became fertile, Ashja' moved to the lands of Banu Damrah. When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله heard of their movement to Banu Damrah, he prepared to march to Ashja' to raid them because of the truce between him and Banu Damrah. Then Allah revealed: "They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved, so that you may be all alike. So do not take allies from among them until they migrate in the way of Allah. But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and do not take from among them any ally or helper."

Then He excepted Ashja', saying: "excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have given them mastery over you, and they would have surely fought you. So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any way [of sanction] against them."

The dwellings of Ashja' were al-Bayda, al-Hull, and al-Mustabah. They had drawn near to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and feared, due to their proximity, that he would send someone to raid them. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had also feared they might strike at his outskirts, so he intended to march toward them. While he was in that state, Ashja' arrived with their leader, Mas'ud ibn Rujaylah, numbering seven hundred. They encamped in the valley of Sal', and that was in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal in the sixth year of the Hijrah. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله called Usayd ibn Husayn and said to him: "Go with a group of your companions until you see what has brought Ashja'."

Usayd went out with three of his companions and stood before them, asking: "What has brought you?" Mas'ud ibn Rujaylah, the leader of Ashja', stood up, greeted Usayd and his companions, and said: "We have come to make a truce with Muhammad." Usayd returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "The people feared I would raid them, so they wanted peace between me and them." Then he sent them ten loads of dates, presenting them before him, and said: "What a fine thing is a gift before a need." Then he came to them and said: "O people of Ashja', what has brought you?" They said: "Our dwellings are near you, and there is no tribe among our people fewer in number than us. We are constrained from fighting you due to our proximity to you, and we are constrained from fighting our people due to our small number among them. So we have come to make a truce with you." The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله accepted from them and made a truce with them. They stayed for their day and then returned to their lands. Concerning them, this verse was revealed: "excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty... (to His saying) then Allah does not allow you any way [of sanction] against them."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from al-Fadl Abu al-Abbas from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you or to fight their own people," he said: "It was revealed concerning Banu Mudlij, for they came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'Our hearts are reluctant to testify that you are the Messenger of Allah, so we are neither with you nor with our people against you.'" He said: I asked: "What did the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله do with them?" He said: "He made a truce with them until he finished with the Arabs, then he would call them; if they responded, [well and good], otherwise he would fight them."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Sayf ibn 'Amirah, he said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about: "to fight you or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have given them mastery over you, and they would have surely fought you." He said: "My father used to say: It was revealed concerning Banu Mudlij; they withdrew and did not fight the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, nor were they with their people." I asked: "What did he do with them?" He said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not fight them until he finished with his enemy, then he renounced [the treaty] to them on equal terms." He said: "And 'their hearts being reluctant' (حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ) means constriction."

In *al-Majma'*: It is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said: "The meaning of His saying, the Exalted: 'a people between whom and you there is a treaty' refers to Hilal ibn 'Uwaymir al-Sulami, who made a treaty on behalf of his people with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He said in his truce: 'On the condition, O Muhammad, that we do not frighten whoever comes to us, and you do not frighten whoever comes to you.' So Allah forbade interfering with anyone who had a covenant with them."

I say: These meanings and similar ones have been narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through various paths from Ibn Abbas and others.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abu Dawud in his *Nasikh*, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Ibn Abbas: Regarding His saying: "excepting those who join a people..." (the verse), he said: "It was abrogated by *Bara'ah*: 'Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you find them' (9:5)."
